sentences.text: Mm hmm.
It's part of Los Angeles.
Part of Los Angeles City.
Yeah, we have the same mayor.
Mm hmm.
Mm hmm.
All right.
My name is Jerry Gerard.
I was born Gerhard [?
Isi ?] Rosenzweig.
And I was born September 6, 1925, in Westphalia, Germany, in a little town called Bunde.
And however, I only lived there for a short while, and eventually moved to Brandenburg, which is a city 40 miles from Berlin, where I grew up most of my young life until age 13.
I was 2 1/2 years old.
I know that because my father was transferred from two different positions as a Rabbi.
His first one was in Bunde.
The second one was in a small town where-- near there.
I'm trying to remember the town-- I can't right this second.
And from there he went to-- Hameln was the small town, H-A-M-E-L-N.
The famous rat catcher of that town-- there's famous story on that.
And then he was transferred to Brandenburg, where he stayed until the beginning of the end.
My father was a Rabbi.
His name was Josef.
He was 11 years older than my mother.
He was a stern but kind educator and father.

He was a teacher, through and through, and he officiated and conducted services at our synagogue and also was head of the school, the educational school preparing boys for their bar mitzvah and religious lessons daily.
He also officiated at burials.
As Jews were dying, he led the services connected with that.
And he also was in charge of the butcher process-- the Jewish way, the Kosher way, where he went to a particular place where animals were prepared for slaughter.
He was a-- that's right.
He was a Mashgiach.
And that didn't last very long, because in 1935, I believe, the law was passed in Germany where killing animals that way was illegal.
Hitler determined, along with the rest of the Germans, that that was not permissible.
He was a cantor, as well.
And what else was he?
He was a-- he went to prisons.
And there was, particularly, one prison near us, where he visited Jews that were political and/or other prisoners in that prison to be of spiritual help to them.
In German, that expression is called a Seelsorger, which means a guide taking charge of the soul.
And that was another one of his occupations.
And I remember watching him do master graphs for tombstone engravings that were done by masons.
And he prepared the master, usually in parchment, that was laid over the, the marble so that it could be engraved in Hebrew letters and whatever other designs were on a tombstone.
He was an artist.
And he had oil paintings of his, which he started doing when he went to school during his studies.
His full name is Josef-- I don't know what his middle name was.
I don't think he had a middle name.
I don't remember it.
Josef Rosenzweig.
Rabbi Josef Rosenzweig-- J-O-S-E-F.
In Germany, Josef was spelled with an F instead of a PH.
He was an orthodox Rabbi, but he was not a Hasid.
By that, I mean he was not so strict that he would wear the typical clothes that Hasidim are wearing-- like Lubavitchs.
He was a modern orthodox Rabbi, if that word means anything.
And he did observe-- we had a kosher household, and we observed all the holidays.
And just observing-- you know, at the same time, by the way, in the '30s-- in the early '30s-- the Leo Baeck and reformed Judaism was already formed.
And I remember my father having or voicing strong objections to that kind of a movement.
Ritual functions.
Yes, he also was a Mohel.
That's correct.
And the congregation in Brandenburg was, if I remember correctly, around 500, 600 families.
So obviously, there were births, besides death.
And he would lead the bris milah, consisting of that.
And it wasn't necessarily done in the hospital.
It was done outside.
My mother was Gertrude Rosenzweig.
She was born Stern, S-T-E-R-N, came from Hanover, Germany.
They met-- she was 11 years younger than my father, and she was a housewife.
And they met, actually, in religious school in the town that I was born in, Bunde, where she was a student, and he was a teacher.
Well, after she got married, she excelled in cooking.
She was particularly good in cooking.
But her occupation really didn't develop until after the war when she came to this country.
I'm jumping the gun, here.
She was a pretty well known caterer and did catering for usually private families-- not commercial catering, but private homes.
That's right.
She was a housewife and a Rebbetzin.
I had a sister, who was three years older than me.
And as a matter of fact, she went to school in Berlin.
She took a-- in the mid '30s-- she took a commuter train from Brandenburg, the 40 miles or so, to Berlin, daily.
And after the laws were passed where you could not get kosher meat or could not-- it was illegal-- Berlin was a place where you could buy it on the black market.

And my sister often brought, towards the end of the week, she brought the weekly meat ration with her, which we purchased, you might say, on the black market, and which was expensive, but it was kosher meat.
Her name was Hannelore.
H-A-N-N-E-L-O-R-E.
Hannelore.
Yes.
I went to public school at age five.
I entered school about six months early.
I was ready, I guess.
And otherwise, I would have been six months late.
So the public school was within walking distance from our home.
And I was there until age 10.
So I was there about five years, and I was a good student.
I was attracted to learning, writing, and arithmetic.
And in 1930-- let me see, 1930, I guess-- I entered the-- it couldn't have been 1930-- 1935, I entered the public school.
And in 1935, I entered the Realgymnasium, which is a secondary school, which was private.
It was paid.
It was not a compulsory-type high school, like we have it here.
And I was in that school until the famous Crystal Night in 1938.
I was the only Jew in that school.
Realgymnasium was a school of at least 1,000, maybe 2,000 students, and all boys.
It was not co-ed.
And I was the only school in the whole school.
I mean, only Jew in the whole school.
As a matter of fact, the classmates in my class, we advanced four years.
I entered at the Sexta, which was the first year, and ended at the Unter Tertia.
The classmates that I got to know in that school would protect whatever antisemitism would come from other older boys in that school, and it was always coming.
And they would stand up for me.
So that was, in a way, quite a consolation, and it made it more palatable.
I belonged to the Maccabi Hatzair.
I think they met like twice a week in the afternoon.
And as a matter of fact, they met in the large common room we had in our house which was located in front of the synagogue, where the Jewish school lessons also took place.
Whenever there wasn't a school lesson, Maccabi Hatzair would meet there.
I was in, I think I was in-- 10, 11 years old, and preteen.
And we learned about-- in those years we learned about Aliya.
We learned about Jews going to preparatory farms to learn how to harvest and work the land, which was, at that time, beginning to take place in Israel.
I personally was never attracted to it, having been influenced by my dad, who explained to me, and to the rest of the family, and to others, that he felt that that was not the solution.
He was an old fashioned Jew, who did not believe that Israel was the answer to the future of Judaism.
This is hard to make a statement of, but a lot of people felt that way at the time.
They felt that the assimilation of Jews in countries was possible.
He had faith in that.
It was a building directly in front of the synagogue, separated by a small courtyard to the synagogue.
And we had a two-story house.
It was the size of a house with a basement, where I had lived and grew up from age 2 and 1/2 until age 13.
It had a formal dining room, and a formal sitting room or salon, and a kitchen.
And it had upstairs bedrooms, and it had a, had the downstairs common room, or where the education took place.
It was actually in our house.
It didn't have a name.
It was the [GERMAN SPEECH].
There was just one.
No, there weren't really any real Jewish neighborhoods.
The town of Brandenburg, I think, had, if I'm not mistaken, about 50,000 people.
It's fairly small.
And there were merchants there.

There was a large contingent of Eastern European Jews that lived and had come there not that long ago from Poland and from ghettos and that always took part in the service, sometimes even led the service, even though their habits of praying were different than the ones that were accepted by the Western and Central European Jews.
They would, they would cover themselves in the tallit, which Western European Jews wouldn't do.
And they would have, you know, the other pronunciation of Hebrew.
But my father encouraged-- for instance, on Yom Kippur, two of the Eastern European Jews who lived in our town would help conduct the service, because no one person can do it all day long, anyway.
I would always fast, by the way.
You mean, as a child?
Speaking of-- yeah.
Yom Kippur.
Yeah.
There were, there were-- especially, when I-- very often, I would spend a summer in my aunt's home about 500 miles away from us near Frankfurt, southwest from where we lived.
And I happened to like her-- my father's sister.
And in that community, a small town called Limburg-- L-I-M-B-U-R-G-- there was antisemitism.
It was a Catholic town.
And they knew, somehow-- the kids knew that I was a Jew.
And they would sometimes run after me and try and beat me up.
And it was rampant.
It was something I grew up with, but somehow managed to deal with.
Brandenburg, less.

Even though there was antisemitism, I was usually-- I was, when I was on the street, I was either with friends or with more than myself, so that it wasn't just being-- they weren't able to single me out as an individual as easily as when I was, for instance, on vacation in that city that I described just now.
Most of my friends were Jewish.
That's right.
They were all Jews, some of them from better known families in the city.
And we also learned-- there were some that were my age, and we learned the preparation of the bar mitzvah together, more or less together.

And there was, particularly, one friend who I was very close with who was picked up, incidentally, in 1938, right after Crystal-- it was before Crystal Night-- when they picked up Eastern European Jews in Germany and brought them back to Poland.
I had never seen him again.
I heard that he survived, though.

My father, because of his past in Germany-- meaning that he was a soldier in the first World War, an officer, and even had an Iron Cross, he was decorated, he had lost part of his hearing in one ear-- was known by the local authorities and was given quite a bit of authority to take care of Jews in need, and to help Jews emigrate.
And he did that.
My mother and he often would discuss, why not leave ourselves?
When was this?
In the mid '30s.
And she had been eager at the time, too.
She saw the handwriting on the wall and urged him to consider leaving the country.
But he had a lot of faith in the future of-- he didn't see the threat of Hitler as much as he-- well, maybe he wasn't being realistic.
It didn't appear to him as such a big threat.
He had so much confidence in the country, as such.

At one time, my mother came up with a adopting process, or a foster family, that she located in New York, and pointed out to my father that she could send me to that family, and then maybe, I could continue to go to school there.
And then when my father found out that they were not kosher, he just nixed the whole thing.
So I was pretty close, there, in the mid '30s to being sent to the United States, if it hadn't been for that.
Besides, he didn't want me to leave the family.
Yes.
We were a closely knit family.
My mother played bridge at least once a week.
She was active with the other Jewish ladies in the city.
I think, among her friends, she also had some non-Jewish ladies.
And we had family in Berlin.
And she would very often travel to Berlin, which was just like an hour's train ride away and spend time with her family, there.
That family was not kosher, and my father hardly ever visited there, although I had.
They were well-to-do people.
They had a summer home near a lake not far from Berlin.
And they had boats, and they had some great recreational activities.
I had a very pleasant life, certainly, a bourgeois standard, or a middle class, if not upper middle class, standard.
Well, the first thing that happened, really, was Sukkot, Sukkot of 1938-- the Succah was still up-- is when my father-- there, there were the-- police came to the house.
It was in early October of 1938.
Police came to the house with a warrant, address warrant-- arrest warrant-- and my father was being taken to the local police station.
And it turned out, subsequently, we learned that somebody had called in to say that he killed chickens the kosher way, which was against the law, which he did.
Oh yes, he did.
But he, you know, it was, it was something that everybody knew, but he was turned into the authorities.
And we expected him to just be given a slap on the hand and be excused.
Well, instead he was convicted to six months in prison, which was a shock, especially to my mother.
As a matter of fact, she had the table set for the day he was in court with his attorney, to have a party at 4 o'clock in the afternoon.
We have it set with our finest china, and all, to celebrate his return.
He had been gone about a week in the police station.
Well, he didn't come back.
And he was sent to jail, and that was a shock.

And it was soon after that-- when she realized my father was in prison for six months, couldn't do much about it-- that she then sent me to a home in Berlin, first to my family in Berlin, and then to a home.
It was actually an orphanage, because she felt that her activities were such that she would do better in not encumbering herself with her children.
--in trying to help my father.
And she saw handwriting on the wall, which was validated by November 9th, I guess-- is when the Crystal Night occurred.
And my mother and my sister were home.
I did not witness it because I was in Berlin at an aunt's house, when they destroyed and burned the synagogue, or the temple and ransacked our home, although they did not burn it.
And that was really the first violent, concerted effort on behalf of the Germans to show Jews-- hey, you guys, you're not wanted here.
It was unfortunate that it had to come to that particular demonstration for people to realize that Jews should leave Germany, and-- although a lot of them already had.

And I would say the exodus of German Jews from, from Germany started in mid-- in the mid '30s. 1935, 1936, 1937 is when people started to, you know, either sell their business or even leave it and leave, knowing that things were not getting any better and more bearable.
My father was helping these people leave, but he himself felt that he was not in danger.
Anyway, now he was in jail.
And his morale was pretty low.
He also had a double hernia in that jail, so he had a physical disability.
And soon my sister-- in December of that year, my sister left to a foster family in England.
And I remember her coming.
By that time, I was in this orphanage where my family sent me in Berlin, [GERMAN SPEECH], which was a well-known, established, old orphanage of very orthodox, and all boys.
And she came to say goodbye.
I didn't really know what was happening.
I was still only 13 years old, barely.
And she said, well, you know, I'll see you.
I'm going to London, and then I'm going to stay in the neighborhood of London.
This was in early December of 1938.
In April of 1939, my father was released from his prison sentence of-- end period.
He came out and was given 48 hours to leave Germany, which was the second major blow to him.
The handwriting was not only on the wall, it was indelible by then.
He had no way, neither emotionally nor financially, to just leave.

So my mother, again, went about to acquire a passport that he needed, an exit visa, and tickets to leave-- first to Paris, and then from Marseilles, the port in southern France, to take a ship to Bolivia, which was the only thing she could get.

What she didn't realize-- and I say this in hindsight, because I didn't know it then-- is that the only way that could have happened smoothly is if she would have planned to go with him, but she did not.
And so by sending him, in that April, 1938, to France with a ticket to take a train in Marseilles, she also did great harm to that relationship-- their, their-- it pointed it out to me later on.
It might be that, at that point, their marriage also was not that good, so my father got a double blow.
He didn't know it, at first.
He didn't know that she wasn't planning on coming, but he was given the 48 hours, and therefore, he had to-- the Germans were very strict.
He had to just leave.
I was still in the orphanage, and I did not see my father before he left.
I had never visited him in prison.

I was to see him, though, soon, because in late June of 1939, a transport was arranged for me and the other kids, many of the other kids in that orphanage, to go up to Paris to be taken care of by way of the O.S.E-- O-S-E.
O period, S period, E period, which is an organization.
It stands for Oeuvre de Secours aux Enfants-- Organization of Help to Children, which, actually, originally was formed in Russia.
A Jewish organization.
And they had prepared a home for this group of kids from Berlin near Paris in June of 19-- end of June of 1939, where we came.
And it was a beautiful summer, by the way, that year.
And I remember the garden.
This was a small, almost like a residence-- there were 40 of us-- a large residence.
There was a French gardener and a few other staff as we arrived there.
